title: Data Structures - Organize Your Data
description: Master lists, dictionaries, sets, and tuples with active AI tutoring

reading_too_long.message: 👋 Hey! I see you're learning about {topic}. Ready to create your first {data_structure}? Let me give you a hands-on exercise!

stuck_on_error.message: 🔍 I notice you're hitting the same error with {data_structure}. The issue is: {error_explanation}. Want me to show you the fix?

confusion_between_structures.message: 🤔 I see you're using a {wrong_type}, but for this task a {correct_type} would work better! Here's why: {explanation}

quick_success.message: ⚡ Impressive! You're mastering data structures fast. Ready for a real-world challenge?

indexing_error.message: 📍 IndexError! Remember: Python indexes start at 0, and the last item is at index -1. The list has {length} items, so valid indexes are 0 to {length-1}.

key_error.message: 🔑 KeyError! That key doesn't exist in your dictionary. Use .get() method or check with 'key in dict' first. Want me to show you?

lessons.title: Data Structures - Your Toolbox!
lessons.ai_welcome_message: 👋 Welcome to Data Structures! Think of these as different containers for organizing your data. Lists for ordered items, dictionaries for key-value pairs, sets for unique items, and tuples for unchangeable data. Let's dive in!

theory.introduction: Data structures are like different types of containers - each designed for specific jobs. Lists are like shopping carts (ordered, can change), dictionaries are like phonebooks (name → number), sets are like unique collections (no duplicates), and tuples are like sealed packages (can't change).
theory.why_important: Choosing the right data structure makes your code faster, cleaner, and easier to understand. It's like using the right tool for the job - you wouldn't use a hammer to cut wood!

key_concepts.explanation: Lists store multiple items in order. You can add, remove, and change items. Perfect for to-do lists, shopping lists, or any sequence of items.
key_concepts.example:
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'orange']
fruits.append('mango')  # Add item
print(fruits[0])        # Access by index: 'apple'
fruits[1] = 'grape'     # Change item
key_concepts.ai_hint: 💡 Lists use square brackets [] and keep items in the order you add them!

key_concepts.explanation: Dictionaries store data as key-value pairs, like a real dictionary (word → definition). Super fast for lookups!
key_concepts.example:
student = {
    'name': 'Emma',
    'age': 20,
    'grade': 'A'
}
print(student['name'])  # Access by key: 'Emma'
student['age'] = 21     # Update value
key_concepts.ai_hint: 🎯 Dictionaries use curly braces {} with colons : between keys and values!

key_concepts.explanation: Sets automatically remove duplicates and don't keep order. Perfect for finding unique items or checking membership super fast!
key_concepts.example:
numbers = {1, 2, 3, 2, 1}  # Duplicates removed
print(numbers)  # {1, 2, 3}
numbers.add(4)  # Add item
print(3 in numbers)  # Check membership: True
key_concepts.ai_hint: ⚡ Sets use curly braces {} but without keys - just values!

key_concepts.explanation: Tuples are like lists but can't be changed after creation. Use them for data that shouldn't change, like coordinates or RGB colors.
key_concepts.example:
point = (10, 20)  # x, y coordinates
color = (255, 0, 0)  # RGB red
print(point[0])  # Access: 10
# point[0] = 15  # ERROR! Can't change tuples
key_concepts.ai_hint: 🔒 Tuples use parentheses () and are immutable (unchangeable)!

key_concepts.explanation:
• List: Ordered items you might change (shopping list, scores)
• Dict: Quick lookups by key (contacts, settings)
• Set: Unique items, no order (unique visitors, tags)
• Tuple: Fixed data that won't change (coordinates, dates)
key_concepts.example:
# Right tool for the job!
todo_list = ['code', 'test', 'deploy']  # List: ordered tasks
contacts = {'Alice': '123-456'}  # Dict: fast lookup
unique_tags = {'python', 'AI', 'web'}  # Set: no duplicates
rgb_red = (255, 0, 0)  # Tuple: color won't change
key_concepts.ai_hint: 🛠️ Think about your needs: Order? Duplicates? Changes? Then pick!

ai_reading_checkpoints.message: 📚 You're learning fast! Want to pause and create your first list or dictionary?
🤔 That's a lot of theory! Let's make it real. I'll give you a fun exercise with real data!

visual_diagram.title: Data Structures Visualized
visual_diagram.content:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  DATA STRUCTURES COMPARISON                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📋 LIST (Mutable, Ordered, Allows Duplicates)
   [0]    [1]    [2]    [3]
   ┌───┐  ┌───┐  ┌───┐  ┌───┐
   │'A'│  │'B'│  │'A'│  │'C'│
   └───┘  └───┘  └───┘  └───┘
   Can: append, insert, remove, sort
   Use: When order matters

📖 DICTIONARY (Key → Value)
   ┌─────────────┐
   │ 'name'  →  'Emma'    │
   │ 'age'   →  25        │
   │ 'city'  →  'London'  │
   └─────────────┘
   Fast lookup by key!
   Use: When you need quick access

🎯 SET (Unique, Unordered)
   ┌───────────────┐
   │  {1, 2, 3, 5} │  ← No duplicates!
   └───────────────┘
   If you add 2 again, it stays the same
   Use: For unique items only

🔒 TUPLE (Immutable, Ordered)
   (x, y, z)
   (10, 20, 30)  ← Can't change!
   
   point = (10, 20)
   point[0] = 15  ❌ ERROR!
   Use: For data that shouldn't change


🎯 QUICK DECISION TREE:

   Need to change items?
         |
    YES  |  NO → Use TUPLE
         |
   Need order?
         |
    YES  |  NO → Use SET (for unique)
         |
   Need key-value?
         |
    YES  |  NO → Use LIST
         |
   Use DICTIONARY
visual_diagram.ai_diagram_prompt: 👀 Looking at the structures? Want me to show you a live example of each? I can create real data and show how they work!

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Shopping List (List)
complete_examples.description: Manage a shopping list with add, remove, and check operations
complete_examples.code:
# Create shopping list
shopping_list = ['milk', 'bread', 'eggs']

print('Original list:', shopping_list)

# Add items
shopping_list.append('cheese')
shopping_list.insert(1, 'butter')  # Insert at position 1

print('After adding:', shopping_list)

# Remove items
shopping_list.remove('bread')  # Remove by value
last_item = shopping_list.pop()  # Remove and return last

print('After removing:', shopping_list)
print('Removed item:', last_item)

# Check if item exists
if 'milk' in shopping_list:
    print('Don\'t forget the milk!')

# List length
print(f'Total items: {len(shopping_list)}')
complete_examples.output:
Original list: ['milk', 'bread', 'eggs']
After adding: ['milk', 'butter', 'bread', 'eggs', 'cheese']
After removing: ['milk', 'butter', 'eggs']
Removed item: cheese
Don't forget the milk!
Total items: 3
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
💡 Lists are perfect for shopping! Notice how we can:
• append() - add to end
• insert() - add at specific position
• remove() - delete by value
• pop() - remove and return last item
• 'in' operator - check if item exists
complete_examples.ai_challenge: 🎯 Try adding 'apples' at the beginning and sorting the list alphabetically!

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Student Records (Dictionary)
complete_examples.description: Store and access student information efficiently
complete_examples.code:
# Create student record
student = {
    'name': 'Emma Watson',
    'age': 20,
    'major': 'Computer Science',
    'gpa': 3.8,
    'courses': ['Python', 'Data Structures', 'Algorithms']
}

print('Student Info:')
print(f"Name: {student['name']}")
print(f"Major: {student['major']}")
print(f"GPA: {student['gpa']}")

# Add new information
student['graduation_year'] = 2025
student['email'] = 'emma@university.edu'

# Update existing
student['age'] = 21
student['gpa'] = 3.9

# Safe access with .get()
phone = student.get('phone', 'Not provided')
print(f"Phone: {phone}")

# List all keys and values
print('\nAll fields:', list(student.keys()))
print(f"Total courses: {len(student['courses'])}")
complete_examples.output:
Student Info:
Name: Emma Watson
Major: Computer Science
GPA: 3.8
Phone: Not provided

All fields: ['name', 'age', 'major', 'gpa', 'courses', 'graduation_year', 'email']
Total courses: 3
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
📖 Dictionaries are like filing cabinets - super fast to find what you need!
• Use square brackets student['key'] to access
• Use .get() for safe access (returns default if key missing)
• Can store any type: strings, numbers, even lists!
• Perfect for structured data like records
complete_examples.ai_challenge: 🔥 Add a 'grades' key with a dictionary of course:grade pairs!

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Unique Visitors (Set)
complete_examples.description: Track unique website visitors, removing duplicates automatically
complete_examples.code:
# Track visitor IPs (some will be duplicates)
visitor_ips = [
    '192.168.1.1',
    '192.168.1.5',
    '192.168.1.1',  # Duplicate
    '192.168.1.10',
    '192.168.1.5',  # Duplicate
    '192.168.1.20'
]

print(f'Total visits: {len(visitor_ips)}')

# Convert to set to get unique visitors
unique_visitors = set(visitor_ips)

print(f'Unique visitors: {len(unique_visitors)}')
print('Visitor IPs:', unique_visitors)

# Add new visitor
unique_visitors.add('192.168.1.30')

# Try adding duplicate (won't be added)
unique_visitors.add('192.168.1.1')

print(f'After new visits: {len(unique_visitors)} unique')

# Check if specific visitor came
if '192.168.1.5' in unique_visitors:
    print('This visitor has been here before!')

# Set operations
today_visitors = {'192.168.1.1', '192.168.1.5', '192.168.1.50'}
yesterday_visitors = {'192.168.1.1', '192.168.1.10', '192.168.1.60'}

# Visitors who came both days
regular_visitors = today_visitors & yesterday_visitors
print(f'\nRegular visitors: {regular_visitors}')

# Visitors only today
new_today = today_visitors - yesterday_visitors
print(f'New today: {new_today}')
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
🎯 Sets are PERFECT for:
• Removing duplicates automatically
• Fast membership testing (in operator)
• Mathematical set operations (union, intersection, difference)

Set operations:
• & (intersection) - items in both
• | (union) - items in either
• - (difference) - items in first but not second
complete_examples.ai_challenge: 💪 Create sets for different user roles and find admins who are also moderators!

complete_examples.title: Example 4: GPS Coordinates (Tuple)
complete_examples.description: Store immutable location data with tuples
complete_examples.code:
# Define city locations (latitude, longitude)
locations = {
    'London': (51.5074, -0.1278),
    'Paris': (48.8566, 2.3522),
    'New York': (40.7128, -74.0060),
    'Tokyo': (35.6762, 139.6503)
}

# Access coordinates
london_coords = locations['London']
lat, lon = london_coords  # Tuple unpacking

print(f'London coordinates: {london_coords}')
print(f'Latitude: {lat}, Longitude: {lon}')

# Calculate distance (simplified)
def distance_label(city1, city2):
    lat1, lon1 = locations[city1]
    lat2, lon2 = locations[city2]
    
    # Simple difference (not real distance)
    diff = abs(lat1 - lat2) + abs(lon1 - lon2)
    
    if diff < 10:
        return 'Close'
    elif diff < 50:
        return 'Moderate'
    else:
        return 'Far'

print(f'\nLondon to Paris: {distance_label("London", "Paris")}')
print(f'London to Tokyo: {distance_label("London", "Tokyo")}')

# RGB colors as tuples
red = (255, 0, 0)
green = (0, 255, 0)
blue = (0, 0, 255)

print(f'\nRed RGB: {red}')
print(f'Green RGB: {green}')

# Tuples are immutable
# red[0] = 200  # This would cause an ERROR!
complete_examples.output:
London coordinates: (51.5074, -0.1278)
Latitude: 51.5074, Longitude: -0.1278

London to Paris: Close
London to Tokyo: Far

Red RGB: (255, 0, 0)
Green RGB: (0, 255, 0)
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
🔒 Tuples are great when data shouldn't change:
• GPS coordinates (fixed location)
• RGB colors (standard values)
• Date/time stamps
• Function return values (multiple items)

Benefits:
• Faster than lists
• Can be dictionary keys (lists can't!)
• Protect data from accidental changes
complete_examples.ai_challenge: 🌍 Add more cities and create a function to find the closest city to a given coordinate!

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Real-World - Contact Book
complete_examples.description: Combine lists and dictionaries for a practical application

complete_examples.ai_explanation:
🎯 Real applications combine structures:
• List of dictionaries for records
• Sets for tags (no duplicates)
• Tuples for fixed data

This pattern is used EVERYWHERE:
• User accounts
• Product catalogs
• Social media posts
• Game inventories
complete_examples.ai_challenge: 🔥 Add a function to add new contacts and another to update phone numbers!

practice_exercises.title: Grade Manager - Lists
practice_exercises.description: Create a program to manage student grades using lists. Calculate average and find highest/lowest grades.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: 🎯 Let's manage grades with lists! This is practical - you'll learn list operations while building something useful.
practice_exercises.starter_code:
# Student grades
grades = [85, 92, 78, 95, 88, 76, 90]

print('All grades:', grades)

# Calculate average
average = # Your code here (sum of grades / number of grades)

# Find highest grade
highest = # Your code here (use max() function)

# Find lowest grade
lowest = # Your code here (use min() function)

# Count grades above 80
above_80 = # Your code here (loop through and count)

print(f'Average: {average:.1f}')
print(f'Highest: {highest}')
print(f'Lowest: {lowest}')
print(f'Grades above 80: {above_80}')

hints.message:
💭 Start with average:
   Average = sum of all grades ÷ number of grades
   
   In Python:
   average = sum(grades) / len(grades)

📊 Great! Now for highest and lowest:
   Python has built-in functions:
   highest = max(grades)
   lowest = min(grades)

📝 Here's the complete solution:

average = sum(grades) / len(grades)
highest = max(grades)
lowest = min(grades)

# Count above 80
above_80 = 0
for grade in grades:
    if grade > 80:
        above_80 += 1

Or use: above_80 = sum(1 for g in grades if g > 80)

practice_exercises.solution:
grades = [85, 92, 78, 95, 88, 76, 90]

print('All grades:', grades)

average = sum(grades) / len(grades)
highest = max(grades)
lowest = min(grades)
above_80 = sum(1 for grade in grades if grade > 80)

print(f'Average: {average:.1f}')
print(f'Highest: {highest}')
print(f'Lowest: {lowest}')
print(f'Grades above 80: {above_80}')

ai_on_run.success: 🎉 Perfect! You've mastered list operations! sum(), len(), max(), min() - these are super useful!
ai_on_run.error: 🔍 Oops! {error_message}. Let me help you fix it...

practice_exercises.ai_after_completion:
💪 Excellent! You learned:
✓ sum() and len() for calculations
✓ max() and min() for finding extremes
✓ Looping through lists
✓ Counting with conditions

Ready for dictionaries?

follow_up_challenge.message: 🔥 BONUS: Add functionality to find what percentage of grades are A's (90+)!

practice_exercises.title: User Profile - Dictionary
practice_exercises.description: Create and manipulate a user profile using dictionaries. Add, update, and display information.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: 📖 Let's build a user profile system! Dictionaries are perfect for this - just like real social media profiles.
practice_exercises.starter_code:
# Create user profile
user_profile = {
    'username': 'coder_123',
    'email': 'user@example.com',
    'age': 25
}

print('Initial Profile:')
for key, value in user_profile.items():
    print(f'{key}: {value}')

# Add new fields
user_profile['location'] = # Your code here (add a city)
user_profile['interests'] = # Your code here (add a list of hobbies)

# Update existing field
user_profile['age'] = # Your code here (increase age by 1)

# Safe access
bio = user_profile.get('bio', 'No bio yet')
print(f'\nBio: {bio}')

# Display updated profile
print('\nUpdated Profile:')
for key, value in user_profile.items():
    print(f'{key}: {value}')

hints.message:
🔄 To update age:
   user_profile['age'] = user_profile['age'] + 1
   Or shorter: user_profile['age'] += 1

👀 Complete solution:

user_profile['location'] = 'London'
user_profile['interests'] = ['coding', 'reading', 'hiking']
user_profile['age'] += 1

bio = user_profile.get('bio', 'No bio yet')

practice_exercises.solution:
user_profile = {
    'username': 'coder_123',
    'email': 'user@example.com',
    'age': 25
}

print('Initial Profile:')
for key, value in user_profile.items():
    print(f'{key}: {value}')

user_profile['location'] = 'London'
user_profile['interests'] = ['coding', 'reading', 'hiking']
user_profile['age'] += 1

bio = user_profile.get('bio', 'No bio yet')
print(f'\nBio: {bio}')

print('\nUpdated Profile:')
for key, value in user_profile.items():
    print(f'{key}: {value}')

ai_progressive_feedback.after_adding_fields: 👍 Great! You added new fields. Now update the age!
ai_progressive_feedback.after_update: 💪 Perfect! Now use .get() for safe access!

practice_exercises.ai_after_completion:
🏆 Brilliant work! You now know:
✓ Creating dictionaries
✓ Adding new key-value pairs
✓ Updating values
✓ Safe access with .get()
✓ Iterating with .items()

Next: Sets!

practice_exercises.title: Tag Manager - Sets
practice_exercises.description: Manage blog post tags using sets. Find common tags, unique tags, and popular tags.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: 🎯 Let's use sets for tag management! This is exactly how blogs and social media work.
practice_exercises.starter_code:
# Blog post tags
post1_tags = {'python', 'programming', 'tutorial', 'beginner'}
post2_tags = {'python', 'data-science', 'tutorial', 'advanced'}
post3_tags = {'javascript', 'web-dev', 'tutorial', 'beginner'}

print('Post 1 tags:', post1_tags)
print('Post 2 tags:', post2_tags)
print('Post 3 tags:', post3_tags)

# Find tags in both post1 AND post2
common_tags = # Your code here (use & operator)

# Find tags in post1 OR post2
all_tags = # Your code here (use | operator)

# Find tags only in post1 (not in post2)
unique_to_post1 = # Your code here (use - operator)

# Find all unique tags across all posts
all_unique_tags = # Your code here (combine all three sets)

# Check if 'python' is popular (in at least 2 posts)
python_count = 0
if 'python' in post1_tags:
    python_count += 1
if 'python' in post2_tags:
    python_count += 1
if 'python' in post3_tags:
    python_count += 1

print(f'\nCommon tags (post1 & post2): {common_tags}')
print(f'All tags (post1 | post2): {all_tags}')
print(f'Unique to post1: {unique_to_post1}')
print(f'All unique tags: {all_unique_tags}')
print(f'Python appears in {python_count} posts')

hints.message:
🎯 Set operations:
   & = intersection (in both)
   | = union (in either)
   - = difference (in first, not second)
   
   common_tags = post1_tags & post2_tags

💡 Great! Now for union and difference:
   all_tags = post1_tags | post2_tags
   unique_to_post1 = post1_tags - post2_tags

📊 Complete solution:

common_tags = post1_tags & post2_tags
all_tags = post1_tags | post2_tags
unique_to_post1 = post1_tags - post2_tags
all_unique_tags = post1_tags | post2_tags | post3_tags

The counting logic looks good!

practice_exercises.solution:
post1_tags = {'python', 'programming', 'tutorial', 'beginner'}
post2_tags = {'python', 'data-science', 'tutorial', 'advanced'}
post3_tags = {'javascript', 'web-dev', 'tutorial', 'beginner'}

print('Post 1 tags:', post1_tags)
print('Post 2 tags:', post2_tags)
print('Post 3 tags:', post3_tags)

common_tags = post1_tags & post2_tags
all_tags = post1_tags | post2_tags
unique_to_post1 = post1_tags - post2_tags
all_unique_tags = post1_tags | post2_tags | post3_tags

python_count = 0
if 'python' in post1_tags:
    python_count += 1
if 'python' in post2_tags:
    python_count += 1
if 'python' in post3_tags:
    python_count += 1

print(f'\nCommon tags (post1 & post2): {common_tags}')
print(f'All tags (post1 | post2): {all_tags}')
print(f'Unique to post1: {unique_to_post1}')
print(f'All unique tags: {all_unique_tags}')
print(f'Python appears in {python_count} posts')
practice_exercises.ai_after_completion:
🎊 Outstanding! You mastered set operations!
✓ Intersection (&) - common items
✓ Union (|) - all items
✓ Difference (-) - unique items
✓ Membership testing

Sets are super fast for these operations!

practice_exercises.title: Restaurant Menu - Mixed Structures
practice_exercises.description: Build a restaurant menu system using nested dictionaries and lists. Calculate totals and find items.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: 🍕 Let's build a real restaurant menu! This combines all data structures - just like production systems do!
practice_exercises.starter_code:
# Restaurant menu
menu = {
    'appetizers': [
        {'name': 'Spring Rolls', 'price': 5.99, 'tags': {'vegetarian', 'popular'}},
        {'name': 'Chicken Wings', 'price': 8.99, 'tags': {'popular', 'spicy'}}
    ],
    'mains': [
        {'name': 'Margherita Pizza', 'price': 12.99, 'tags': {'vegetarian'}},
        {'name': 'Beef Burger', 'price': 14.99, 'tags': {'popular'}},
        {'name': 'Veggie Pasta', 'price': 11.99, 'tags': {'vegetarian', 'vegan'}}
    ],
    'desserts': [
        {'name': 'Chocolate Cake', 'price': 6.99, 'tags': {'popular'}},
        {'name': 'Ice Cream', 'price': 4.99, 'tags': set()}
    ]
}

# Display menu
print('🍽️  RESTAURANT MENU')
print('=' * 50)

for category, items in menu.items():
    print(f'\n{category.upper()}:')
    for item in items:
        tags_str = ', '.join(item['tags']) if item['tags'] else 'None'
        print(f"  {item['name']:<20} £{item['price']:.2f}  [{tags_str}]")

# TASK 1: Find all vegetarian items
vegetarian_items = # Your code here

print('\n' + '=' * 50)
print('VEGETARIAN OPTIONS:')
for item in vegetarian_items:
    print(f"  {item['name']} - £{item['price']:.2f}")

# TASK 2: Calculate total if ordering one of each popular item
popular_items = # Your code here
total_cost = # Your code here (sum of prices)

print(f'\nPopular items total: £{total_cost:.2f}')

# TASK 3: Find cheapest item
all_items = # Your code here (combine all lists)
cheapest = # Your code here (use min with key)

print(f'\nCheapest item: {cheapest["name"]} - £{cheapest["price"]:.2f}')

hints.message:
📊 Great! Now for popular items:
   Similar logic, but check for 'popular' tag
   Then sum the prices:
   
   total_cost = sum(item['price'] for item in popular_items)

ai_progressive_feedback.after_vegetarian: 🥗 Perfect filtering! Now find the popular items and calculate total.
ai_progressive_feedback.after_popular: 💰 Great calculation! Last step: find the cheapest item across all categories.
ai_progressive_feedback.all_correct: 🎊 AMAZING! You combined ALL data structures perfectly!

practice_exercises.ai_after_completion:
🏆 WOW! You just built a real system!

You mastered:
✓ Nested dictionaries (menu structure)
✓ Lists of dictionaries (items)
✓ Sets for tags (efficient checking)
✓ Filtering and searching
✓ Calculations across structures
✓ Finding min/max with custom keys

This is EXACTLY how real applications work!

bonus_challenge.title: 🔥 ULTIMATE CHALLENGE
bonus_challenge.description: Add a 'order' function that takes item names and calculates total with 10% service charge

ai_lesson_summary.on_completion:
�� DATA STRUCTURES MASTERED!

You've conquered all four structures!

📋 LISTS
  ✓ Ordered collections
  ✓ Mutable (can change)
  ✓ Allow duplicates
  ✓ Perfect for sequences

📖 DICTIONARIES
  ✓ Key-value pairs
  ✓ Super fast lookups
  ✓ Perfect for structured data
  ✓ Real-world applications

🎯 SETS
  ✓ Unique items only
  ✓ Mathematical operations
  ✓ Lightning fast membership testing
  ✓ Great for deduplication

🔒 TUPLES
  ✓ Immutable (can't change)
  ✓ Faster than lists
  ✓ Safe data storage
  ✓ Perfect for fixed data

Exercises completed: {count}/4
Time spent: {minutes} minutes

You're now ready for Functions & Modules!
